text: A very-high acute:chronic workload ratio (≥2.11) is associated with the greatest risk of injury in the current week (16.7% injury risk) and subsequent week (11.8% injury risk).
High chronic workload combined with a very-high 2-week average acute:chronic workload ratio (≥1.54) produces the greatest injury risk (28.6%).
specific_value: ≥1.54 ACWR with high chronic load, 28.6% injury risk

text: Athletes with high chronic workload are more resistant to injury when maintaining moderate acute:chronic workload ratios (0.85-1.35) compared to athletes with low chronic workload.
High chronic workload combined with a moderate workload ratio (1.02-1.18) carries lower injury risk than low chronic workload combined with several workload ratios (relative risk 0.3-0.7).
Athletes with high chronic workload become less resistant to injury when subjected to acute workload spikes (acute:chronic workload ratios around 1.5 or above).
Considering acute and chronic workloads in isolation (not as ratios) does not consistently predict injury risk; the ratio of acute to chronic workload is a superior predictor.
Building a high chronic workload base has a protective effect against injury, provided acute workload spikes are avoided.
